chunks.text: Welcome. Hello. Do you want anybody to come and join you, or are you in splendid isolation? By myself, yeah. That's the sort of support you need, isn't it?

We'll get everybody in, and then we'll get underway. John Bray. Look, if there's some particularly important people who need to sit around the table, I think we're short of seats at the back rather than around the table.

Okay. Welcome, Minister. Good to have you here. Hello. And hello. And what we usually do is throw over to you for, say, five minutes or so and then rip into questions. But- Well, I won't take five minutes. And I gather you've had some important announcements about now as well. Yes. Well, I just wanted to highlight the fact that government's released the response to the National Infrastructure Plan, which the Infrastructure Commission, Te Waihanga, issued in February this year, and we are statutorily required to respond by the

20th of June. So we've met the law, which is always good, particularly when you're the attorney. Just to pull out a few highlights, I suppose. The first point to make is, I hope the committee agrees, I think you've been briefed on it. The plan released by the commission as a sobering wake-up call for many people, outlines that we spend a lot on infrastructure, about 5.8% of GDP annually over the last 20 years, one of the highest in the OECD, but we don't get a lot of bang for buck. Real structural problems around asset management, for

example. We're fourth to last in the OECD for asset management. Central government agencies don't understand what they own or have long-term investment plans. And the cost of addressing our infrastructure deficit as a country outstrips our ability to pay. So there are 16 recommendations in the plan and 10 priority areas, and the government's response essentially endorses all 16 of the recommendations.

13 recommendations supported, 13 supported in principle. I think it's heartening that a lot of what the government's already doing reflects some of the recommendations in the plan, particularly the priorities. So, things like, for example, up zoning around rapid transit and key transport corridors, lifting hospital investments, spatial planning, national adaptation, time of use pricing, fleet-wide road user charges, those kind of recommendations, all those priorities are existing government policy.

In April, we also announced changes to the investment management system, so to take external investment assurance to the commission, establishing a dedicated function inside the commission for capital-intensive agencies, covering both long-term investment plans and infrastructure asset management.

And then the other things I would pull out in terms of the government response are governments agreed to review the land transport funding system. There's quite a lot of good material in the National Infrastructure Plan around issues with land transport funding settings. We've agreed to do a review, which will be led by MCERT, to be publicly consulted on by June 2028.

We've also agreed to legislate for departments and Crown entities to publish long-term investment plans and to report on asset management. It's likely that will involve legislative amendment to the Public Finance Act and the Crown Entities Act. There are existing requirements through Cabinet Office Circular 23/9. Really rolls off the tongue.

The government's view is that putting that into law makes a lot of sense and will lift the quality of those over time. Done quite a lot of hard work on that in the last two years. We now have long-term investment plans for many government agencies, but we can do more, and having that as a legal requirement, I think will make a difference.

There's also another couple of recommendations about mandating contributions to the national infrastructure pipeline, which I know is a thing that people are interested in strengthening data quality over time. The other point I'd make is that we have deliberately tried to be nonpartisan about this, and I want to thank both Labour and the Greens for offering forewords to the government response.

So got a ministerial foreword from me on behalf of the government and forewords from both Labour Party infrastructure spokesperson and the Green Party infrastructure spokesperson as well. And so, while I don't agree with everything in it, I think it'd be fair to characterize it as broadly agreeing with the direction of travel, both in the plan and the government's response as well.

So I see this as part of fixing the system settings that have led us to a pretty uncomfortable place as a country in terms of infrastructure investment. And this is the kind of, some would say, boring, but really important institutional system reforms so that we do better over time.

And we framed it as a 30-year long-term plan, and if we stick to the plan and implement the recommendations, we'll all be a better, wealthier, more prosperous, and more value for money country in the next 10, 20, and 30 years. So thank you very much. Thank you for that intro. Kieran, you've got the first question. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And thanks very much for that. Credit to you.

I think it might have been this time last year, I said, "Why don't we work together on including an opposition response to the plan," and you've followed through with that. So thanks very much for that. I'm just looking at it now, given it's just come through, and I see the three areas that you support in principle: predictable government funding signals, multi-year budgeting, and the coordinated workforce development.

Could you give us a rundown through those three as to why you're not in a position to commit to the recommendation? The short point is we want to do a bit more work on all of them. We support all of them. It's just, do you want to commit to everything as part of that right now?

So if you take coordinated workforce development, there's obviously a lot of work underway around industry skills boards already. MSD's already using the national infrastructure pipeline. So we've just said in the response, the government will consider how to implement this recommendation by directing MB to work with the commission.

So I suppose, the point is there's just a bit of further work to do before we jump in holus-bolus into exactly what the commission is recommending. So we said, this is a nomenclature question, I suppose. What does supportive mean compared to supportive in principle? We just want to do a bit of further work on that. Thank you. So the work that is signaled on an assurance- Yeah... and we've signaled our support for that as well, there's still some work to do in that.

Yeah. Can you walk us through what's required from transferring from Treasury to the commission and- Yeah... some specific time frames? I'll make a couple of comments, and I might get Jeff and Chris. Sorry, I should have introduced this. Jeff Puke, CEO of Infrastructure Commission, Chris Bunny, DCE at Treasury. So yeah, so we've announced those big five changes, in April. 1st of November is when you take over the assurance functions. There's a bit of work to do between now and then around that.

So I might, Jeff first and then maybe Chris can talk about what that involves. Yeah, so essentially, we're going to be bringing together best aspects of the former gateway process, which high-risk, high-profile projects go through currently. And bring that together with the Infrastructure Priorities Program, and essentially have one product for high-risk, high-profile investments.

We are working through the details of what this means for things outside of central government. You'll recall that local government, the IPP was open to broader. It will start just by being a central government-focused program, but with options to expand it thereafter.

So it's the back end between Treasury and you're working on the- Sure... what the assurance transitionalization looks like. Yeah. So Treasury will be establishing the policy on what is required under the program. So we're working currently with Treasury on what that looks like, what projects are eligible for it, the time frames involved in that, and so on.

And then the commission will essentially be responsible for implementing that policy. I don't know if there's anything you want to add to that. Yeah. Just to add, there's a few practical elements. So, we don't want to cause confusion with agencies because this is assurance for infrastructure projects. There's still digital projects and other change projects as well. There's with Jeff working through the access to the current pool of gateway reviewers, just making sure that you don't drop anything on that in a way. And then practical considerations, like some

infrastructure projects might have security classifications, so just making sure that the settings are such that InfraCom's well-placed and appropriate to pick those projects up as we go. So it's mostly pretty mundane, just detail of making sure that the system works come 1 November in a way that doesn't see balls dropped at that point. Sure.

Okay. Is that a start, or... No, I'm going to go Dan, then Julianne. Thanks, Minister, for coming today. You've just released the government's response to the infrastructure plan. Just keen to get your sense on, yeah, clearly there's 30 year program of work, and it's over successive governments.

Mm-hmm. What's the update that you can provide to the committee around getting a bipartisan agreement around the plan, but also the pipeline of hundreds of projects? Yeah. How do we take the politics out of the infrastructure question and- Yeah. As I said, we've worked pretty hard, and Mr. McNulty's given an indication of that, we've worked pretty hard to try and land a degree of bipartisanship over the system settings.

And I think some of you have heard me say this before, I think we probably tie ourselves up a bit too much over specific projects and who supports X and Y. And there's high-profile disagreements over projects, and one of the ways you can avoid doing that over time is to implement the recommendations of the plan.

So if you have proper long-term investment plans, proper asset management plans, you have a proper assurance function. Politicians don't go and announce projects before they're ready to be announced and before they're ready to be funded. If you can avoid all of that stuff and put in place all the institutional reforms that we're doing through the response to the National Infrastructure Plan, and have already started, frankly, you will lead to less high-profile disagreements over time, and that's really important.

But at a more general level, I do think we probably fixate a bit too much about specific projects like rail or IREX or whatever. Actually, if you look at the national infrastructure pipeline, which is publicly available for anybody to look at, and you can break it down by area, class of activity, funding source, implementation, construction, pre-planning.

There's a wealth of data there. I'd put it to you that 97 to 98% of what's in the pipeline, no one disagrees with. Mm-hmm. There's just water renewal work that has to happen, basic road maintenance, culverts, bridge repairs, bridge replacements, Eastern Busway, next stage has just been funded, that's been rolled out.

That's basically been a 20-year build. Completely uncontroversial because it's a good project, it's been planned, and it's taken too long. It's cost too much. But welcome to New Zealand infrastructure. But it's just been rolling out, and no one really disagrees with it. And so it's the high-profile stuff that we all fixate on, and probably there's too much political argy-bargy over, but I reckon 98% of the stuff in the pipeline is just pretty straightforward.

And one of the points I keep trying to make to people is there is an existing pipeline. Everyone always goes, "Oh, we need a pipeline." It's like, well, there is one. Everyone goes, "We need to be bipartisan." It's like, well, I think that actually basically is bipartisan. Mm-hmm. And let's focus on getting the consensus over the system reforms required.

And that's where I do think we are starting to make some good progress. So that's a positive thing, I think, for the country. Good stuff. Just another question. In addition to the 16 areas, you've made some further actions around four different areas. Do you want to talk us through those and- Oh, well, the 16, the ones I wanted to pull out were the ones, just kind of what I just mentioned, to be honest. So, the land transport funding review is important. The long-term investment plans, strengthening public sector leadership.

Those are the things that I kind of haven't talked about before, and that we haven't previously given an indication on. So, those are important. One of the things I've learned in this job over the last two and a half years or so is that quality people make the system, and I think it'd be fair to say we need greater capability inside the system at a project level. Projects go wrong for lots of reasons.

One big one is the quality of leadership at a individual project level. And so the more we can lift capability inside the Crown when it comes to delivery- You know, that will be important. So there's some recommendations there that we've endorsed. And there's a lot of things still to do around that. Good stuff.

Juliane. Thank you. Thanks, Minister. I wanted to just ask about recommendation two, which is related to land transport- Yeah... funding oversight. Yeah And whether the investment assurance infrastructure priority program will be applied to all major new capital projects in land transport that might be committed to before the election.

So it won't apply to NZTA's NLTF funded user charge program. So, basically where we've got to is 50% of transport spending is through the New Zealand Transport Agency, so their board approved investment decisions made through the National Land Transport Fund, which essentially user pays, and they have oversight over that, and they just make those investment decisions.

So it won't apply to that because, we have NZTA as a Crown entity to make those investment decisions specifically for a reason. Where the Crown decides to invest Crown capital, put more money into a project or series of projects, the new system will apply to those, but it's not up and running till the 1st of November.

Thanks. So if you have a question- After the election, yeah. Well, yeah, as we've just canvassed 10 minutes or so ago, there's a bunch of work to do between now and then. Mm. Yeah. So just following up on that. Because the way I read the recommendation, land transport has been identified in the National Infrastructural Plan as an area where we haven't been spending enough on maintenance and renewals, where we've been committing to lots of large new capital projects.

The government's saying they've supported the National Infrastructural Plan's recommendation, but actually they're not putting major new capital land transport projects through the same process that all other- Well, the-... projects would go through. The, the- Unless it's Crown funded Well, I'm not sure that's fair. We've endorsed the recommendation, which is, if you read pages 64 through 70 of the, the National Infrastructure Plan, it's a pretty sobering read around the last 10 to 15 years of transport funding policy,

and it makes a pretty compelling case for how we've gone awry, and we've endorsed the problems that have been identified by the commission, and we've endorsed doing a review to try and sort it out. So, that is pretty significant. There's a lot of funding- A lot of work to do... going into transport projects and about transport NLTP.

Yes. That's Crown funding. Yes. But so the projects that are tagged to Crown funding will go through that process, but not the ones that are in theory other- Well, the ones in the most recent budget, won't, because the- Okay, yeah, because they're already- You're talking about Cambridge?

Just, yeah. Earlier we were just going through the supplementary estimates. I- And there was quite a large Crown injection to the NLTP last year... Yeah. Those stakes- Like 5 billion over five years... Well, it goes back through- Yeah... really, to be honest, without being too political about it, really started through the upgrade program.

This existed before then, but the upgrade program back in 2020 supercharged it. We now call it MSIP. But this government's put Crown capital into transport too. The reality is, you know this as well as I do, that there's not enough revenue in the system to pay for every thing that people want to do.

Yes. So I'll be really upfront about that. So can I just ask though, there's also a specific recommendation around prioritizing and sequencing land transport projects. Is that something that we will see from the government before the election, or? See what? Like, what the prioritization and sequencing of major land transport projects.

Well, NZTA has that already. Oh, NZTA has already decided the prioritization? NZTA has what's called the National Land Transport Plan. They have a program of that already, and we're now going to do a review of land transport funding in light of the problems identified by the commission.

And will that review be independent from NZTA or? Well, they'll be involved in it, but, yes, it will be. It won't be done by them. We're reviewing them, so it won't be done by them. Okay. But- Though they'll be involved... but is it your expectation that NZTA will continue committing billions of dollars of funding to big capital projects, and sometimes through a PPP, which is going to lock in repayments over 20, 25 years with that prior to this review and prioritization and- Look, the system has to keep operating.

We're not stopping transport investment while we do a review. The system's got to operate. You got to build the plane while you fly it a bit. NZTA is going to keep operating, and the system settings for NZTA are what they are at the moment through the GPS '24 through '27 period. We're just going to start a review, and the scope of that is scope we've indicated, but the who and when and how and all that stuff is yet to be worked through.

So the kind of rationalization of what we're spending on transport won't start until after the GPS 2024 to 2027? Well, there's a new GPS coming. Okay. Okay, I think we've got that one. Tangi, you had a sup Yeah. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Thanks, Minister. I've just seen this today as well, this afternoon.

So you're confirming that the new assurance process will kick in from the 1st of November? Yes, we publicly announced that. That was announced in April. Okay. But there's still an asterisk here around the defense side of things. The plan itself identified transport as a particular focus, and the need for that. So can I just get some clarity? Basically, the RONs.

Yeah Is the expectation that the RONs would be put through this assurance process? If they're Crown funded, yes. Okay. What about partially? Probably, yes. Probably, yes. Okay. But is it like a- Hang on. Sorry, are you finished, Tangi? Sorry, just one further thing. In terms of your earlier comment around some of those decisions are best taken by NZTA, is that because you're relying on them, not saying I agree with this or not, but you're relying on them to be best placed to be making those decisions in the context of what's available?

Yeah. If you read the commissions report, they go on to quite a lot of detail around how this stuff works. So if you go back in time, in 2008, NZTA was created. We brought together Transfund, Transit, and the LTSA into a big beast called NZTA.

Now, that was a decision Annette King made. The view was having it all in one place was better. That's what we've got. The way in which land transport funding works is we set a government policy statement, and the law essentially says to ministers and the government of the day, you can be quite didactic and directive around the funding categories, the activity classes, and where the money goes.

That is quite a contrast from other network utility industries like, say, electricity lines, for example, or electricity and transmission investment, or a range of other industries. Energy is another one. Energy generation, more broadly. But that's the system we have and the reality is that has led to some challenges over successive governments about the way in which we make transport investments.

So, the NZTA board applies the GPS by law, and from the GPS spawns a national land transport plan, which uses a national land transport fund implement in accordance with government policy. And government policy changes and has changed over successive governments, and partly the oscillation and change in government policy priorities has led to a bit of whiplash out there in local government land and in the sector.

And that's problematic, and you know that as well as I do. So what I'm signaling and what the government is signaling in response to the plan is that the status quo is not sustainable, right? In which we have 50% of transport spending in this country as user charges, and the rest of it is just taxpayers. Because all of that Crown capital, all of that taxpayer capital competes with schools and hospitals and defense and courthouses and police stations and everything else the government does.

And there's been a massive investment in transport. The system is meant to be user pays, and it isn't. So I've been really upfront about that. I've been saying that for nine months. You done on this one? Okay. Sup on this? No. No, because I do have a sup on this. So just, are you saying in effect that the GPS is a model which we should be changing? Because you're right- We're doing a review.

Yeah. Well, okay. That'll be pretty useful. Okay. Simon. Julianne, you had a sup on that, too? Yeah. I guess my sup was just about the government has realized, thanks to the National Infrastructure Plan and the work there, that we've been spending too much money on large new capital projects.

But the government's saying that- Respectfully, I've been giving speeches about this since October, so- Yeah... it doesn't take the plan to point that out to me. Right. But thank you. Okay. Yeah. The plan and your work, recognizing that. But it sounds like until this process is in place, there's still room for the government to commit the country to probably more than $10 billion of needing to repay a PPP on one- You worry about Northland... specific project that's not going to go through- You worry about Northland

... this investment assurance process. Well, it's got to go through all the various stages and gateways and meet the affordability threshold from this month. So there will be a invert-- like a- We're committed to Northland. If you're asking about Northland, we're committed to the first stage of what we've did to Tahara. Okay. And so that's irrespective of the concerns that have been signaled about how the country's been spending money on infrastructure.

Wouldn't this be a good time to revisit that- We're not intending to revisit... if we want long-term- We're not intending to revisit what we've- If we want long-term cross-party consensus on- Sure, but we're not-... investing in the very best projects that are going to be- Sure. I know that you don't like Northland, and we have a different view, so. Well, no, I'm not-- That's one thing to say- Okay... I don't like it. It's not been through the process that you're saying that all big capital projects- It's been-... should be through.

Yeah. It's been through an NZTA investment case process. And we know the-- And that is directed by the GPS, which just lists a strategy of building this particular road- Yeah, but that's a bit demeaning to NZTA. It's gone through quite a robust investment case process through NZTA. Okay. I'll ask one more sup on this, and then I'm going to go to Simon, and then I'm going to come back up to Kieran, then Callus.

So as we go through this assurance process If the Infrastructure Commission Treasury say, "We actually don't think that this particular project stacks up," what happens to that project? Well, ultimately, that will be on ministers to make a decision. We're not subcontracting investment decisions to an independent agency, through the commission.

For Crown capital funded projects, we're not doing that. And just lift yourself up from transport for a moment, because I get everyone forgets we're saying about- I wasn't just focused on transport. I was just across- Yeah, okay, good. Because the new investment assurance process is quite a shift, because it will apply to hospitals, schools, courthouses, police stations. Anything that goes through the investment management system, well, with some exceptions, the stuff that goes through the investment management system,

will be subject to that process. And, ultimately, it's cabinet that makes decisions either through budget or through the ordinary run-of-the-mill events. And, again, I've talked publicly about this before, and I'll just repeat it. The Minister of Finance and I in particular have been extremely frustrated at how poor the system is at leading to good decision-making.

And, frankly, some of the projects that are late, delayed, expensive... I'm trying to make this in a way that's not too political. Ministers should have rejected and intervened far earlier than they did in order to get the project back on track.

On the other hand, to be nice to past governments, ministers can only intervene when they have the right information. So that's part of the system we're trying to change. So if ministers are not told that a hospital, for example, has gateway red flags all through it, and that the project is a disaster, it's a bit unfair for ministers to be expected to intervene if they don't know.

Okay? I don't think Erica was before this committee, but she's been on the media this morning on this IT project. Which I'm not totally familiar with at all, but sounds just like an absolute cluster of epic proportions, and which basically ministers were misled. And so that's sort of my point in an infrastructure context is if you don't have the information, you can't step in and do the proper assurance role as ministers and actually withhold money and say, "You know what?

We're not going to keep throwing good money after bad." You need the information. And so what we're trying to build through the system and through the assurance changes is a proper professional system so that people like me, who have to sit around and discharge hundreds of millions of dollars of public money, can actually say yes or no, knowing that with some degree of confidence, that the money's going to go to the right place and the project will actually be built.

That wasn't the situation three years ago when we first became government. It's not the situation now, but we're getting there. We're making improvements. So these guys are the guys to give the red flags, and then it's up to the ministers to take that red flag or not. Yeah. So the other question I've got there, final question from me, and then I'll go to Simon, is just what does this mean in your view for political parties making promises at election time in terms of saying, "I'm going to build this, I'm going to build this, I'm going to build this." Or is it, "I'm going to look at doing

this, I'm going to look at doing this, I'm going to look at doing this. Because I know, and I will get some advice about whether this is good, bad, or indifferent." I don't know what it is. This is not the time to talk about campaign stuff. Oh, yeah, no, I just thought it was useful. Well, I'll just make this point. We all need to be better at not promising things we don't really know much about.

Both sides of politics have been guilty of that. Because it's easy, right? To turn up and say, "We're going to build X and Y." Next question, how much? Oh, I don't know. Next question, when? Oh, I don't know. Next question, when will it finish? Oh, I don't know. So- It's useful... we've all got to be better. We've got to be better.

Good. I like that. Simon. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Minister, we've heard plenty of concerns coming from the engineering community, contractors about gold plating, green plating- Yeah... scope creep in major infrastructure projects. Yeah. So, it costs blow out and where practical alternatives never get considered.

So I'm interested in what work is underway to ensure that business cases test those simpler- Yeah... low cost and standardized options before moving to a bespoke or a higher spec alternative. Question. Yeah. So it's a good question. We've placed a real emphasis on that, and frankly, I think we can go further. Roading, for example, is an area that really makes me anxious.

Just how much stuff costs, and the reality is we are not a wealthy enough country to afford some of the things we want to build, and we need to look at value for money. We've made some really good progress on school property, which is not really within the scope of the estimates, but it's an example of what you're talking about, in which we've gone for much more standardized modular designs.

Erica would be able to give you the numbers, but I think the average cost of a classroom's gone from about 1.2 to 650. Varies a bit, it's an average. It's been in the media a bit this week. It is an average. But there's more we can do on health in that regard. Samian would be the first to say that. I don't know if you saw the most recent police station at Taradale.

It's just opened. That was done through a modular off-site build, and then sent up the road. It was done in the Hutt, actually. Sent up the road to Taradale. It was delivered on time, under budget. It's a modular, standardized design. I think there's more we can do there. I'll give you one example, courthouses.

Yep, they've got some specialty requirements, but they're just courthouses. We try and reinvent the wheel too much and be bespoke about these things. We've just got to get towards... There's a spectrum, but we've got to move towards much more standardization in looking at value for money solutions. We've got a long way to go.

We've made some progress, but there's more to do. Cool. One more. Yeah. Yeah. We've also seen some Crown infrastructure projects struggle with delivery capability. I think you mentioned hospitals, procurement discipline, scope control, of course, and managing cost escalation. So I'm interested, how does the minister see the role for Crown infrastructure delivery- Yeah... in lifting value for money for- Well, I've got John O'Hagan here. So just brief context, and I'll hand over to John.

What we did in '24 was we reorganized the system a bit. So the commission's now in charge of strategy. Treasury's in charge of policy and obviously the fiscal settings. And NFCO's in charge of the private finance stuff, Graham's here, and all of the other things that they do, which is large. And we've got John here from CID. It's the old Ōtakaro.

It's the old Roepaeinga. That's okay. Ōtakaro, then Roepaeinga, then CID. Righty-o. So that's like the Crown specialty infrastructure delivery entity. And so I might ask John to talk about some of their successes. We're making real progress. Yes. So the committee's touched on a couple of things, and you've touched on modular design and construction.

So, Crown infrastructure delivery, we see ourselves not only in the delivery space, but also in the project advisory space as well. And, as you'll be aware, there's some mandated agencies that are mandated to use us, and those agencies are ones that don't typically deliver infrastructure projects.

So instead of them standing up their own delivery entity or personnel within their own organization, they use us for that. So our role is very much around value for money, value engineering, value management, modular construction. So we actually did the short-term enroll growth program with the Ministry of Health for the Auckland projects last year. So that- Education.

Sorry, education. Sorry. So the ones that the minister referred to, we actually did a lot of work there to get that $1.3 million down to $650,000. And we're currently working with Health NZ, doing their interim inpatient units across five hospitals across the country. So again, modular construction. We have got a contractor working out of Auckland, and they're fabricating their complete units to put on the back of the truck.

And, Hawksbay has been a great success there, where we've just landed effectively the whole unit over the course of a couple of weeks, and we're now just into commissioning and finalizing it. So we're embedding ourselves into projects to get value for money, cost savings, and we're looking at courts. Courts of Ministry of Justice are another agency that we work with. Doing a great job with Auckland District Court, where we have saved the ministry a lot of money there.

Very expensive courts. And we're currently working with the ministry on the virtual court. Great. Excellent. Thank you very much. Kieran. Thank you. You said yourself that reasonable people can disagree on the LNG terminal, and in the government's response, you're talking about how important it is that ministers did make informed decisions.

Yeah. Would you hold off the LNG terminal so that it can go through the infrastructure's assurance processes? Well, I'm not responsible for the LNG terminal. It's an issue for Simeon. I can tell you that Cabinet set up a pretty rigorous process around it.

And we've got NFCO leading on... Well, NFCO's involved. Graham might want to talk about his shop's involvement there. Simeon's leading on that, but we're running a pretty rigorous process around it. If the timing was different, though, this is the sort of project that would go through that process, would it not?

I'm not sure it would, because we're not going to own the LNG terminal. So it's only infrastructure that, I speak to the Crown owns- It's going through-... that would go through that insurance process... it's going through a business case process, but it's not... Yeah, so it's an interesting one, isn't it? I don't know if it ordinarily would.

It doesn't go through the investment management system in the ordinary way. Given the level of expenditure that is required and the relative controversy that surrounds this proposal- Yeah... is that not a potential gap in this process, that such a piece of infrastructure wouldn't necessarily go through the process that other- Mm... Crown pieces of infrastructure are?

But don't you want ministers to make informed decisions across the board? Yeah, but I can tell you we are going to make an informed decision. We're running it through a proper process. There's a procurement underway. There's business casing underway. There's- I mean, we are doing it in a rigorous way. I don't know, Graham, do you want to talk about...

Well, he's probably commercial confident. I don't know. Maybe not. But can I just jump in with the- Kieran, have you finished? Just to... This is literally the one project that we, as a party, haven't been able to- Yeah... say, "Yes, we will see this through," amendments, because there's still unanswered questions. The Commission's plan expressed reservations about it.
